refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss limit). The refined FR isolates the quality/reliability obligation in the problem domain without prescribing how (e.g., protocol selection, redundancy, error correction). 'Communication reliability' is solution-neutral language that avoids naming specific technologies. The acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤1% or customer-defined target) is a measurable constraint, not part of the FR itself.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles). Measure end-to-end packet loss across representative mission scenarios. Confirm measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined acceptance threshold. Document network conditions and measurement methodology.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of heavy equipment from a tele-operator station.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback data integrity within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and network condition feedback
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture with automatic failover to maintain continuous connectivity across independent network routes
DP3: Deploy quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to reserve bandwidth for critical teleoperation commands and reduce contention-induced loss
DP4: Integrate forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels, tuned to network conditions and customer acceptance thresholds
DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining selective retransmission for critical commands, FEC for sensor streams, and QoS prioritization across a monitored network path
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining selective retransmission for critical commands, FEC for sensor streams, and QoS prioritization across a monitored network path

potential_sources: Coupling between retransmission logic and FEC encoder/decoder state management—mitigated by clear separation of concerns: retransmission operates on command/control frames; FEC operates on sensor/telemetry streams
Coupling between QoS prioritization and packet classification—managed by defining explicit frame type taxonomy and priority rules decoupled from specific network hardware
Coupling between network monitoring and adaptation triggers—risk of feedback loops; mitigated by defining stable thresholds and hysteresis bands to prevent oscillation
coupling_check.notes: DP5 avoids tight coupling to specific network technologies (e.g., 5G, WiFi, Ethernet) by defining logical mechanisms (retransmission, FEC, QoS) that are transport-agnostic. Coupling is primarily internal (between the three mechanisms) and is manageable through clear interface contracts and state isolation. The design supports independence of each mechanism's tuning and evolution.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion (≤1%) is achievable within the agreed network conditions; if baseline loss exceeds this, no DP can guarantee compliance without network infrastructure upgrade.
ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation command latency tolerance is known and documented; hybrid approach (retransmission + FEC) may introduce variable latency that must remain within acceptable bounds.

ASSUMPTION: Network monitoring infrastructure (loss detection, condition feedback) is available or can be implemented without significant cost/complexity; if not, adaptation triggers become reactive rather than proactive.
RISK: FEC overhead (redundant packets) may consume bandwidth and increase latency; requires careful tuning of redundancy levels per stream type.
RISK: Selective retransmission assumes command frames are idempotent or can tolerate occasional duplication; if not, additional deduplication logic is required.
RISK: QoS prioritization effectiveness depends on network device support; if worksite network lacks QoS capability, this mechanism provides no benefit.
RISK: Hybrid approach increases implementation complexity and testing surface; requires comprehensive validation across diverse network conditions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability by achieving packet loss ≤ [customer-defined target, default 1%] under agreed network conditions, with latency impact bounded by [customer-defined latency budget, e.g., ≤100ms added delay], using a combination of selective retransmission for critical commands, forward error correction for sensor streams, and QoS prioritization. The solution shall be agnostic to specific network transport technologies and shall include configurable tuning parameters to adapt to varying worksite network conditions. Success criteria: (1) Measured end-to-end packet loss meets acceptance threshold in baseline and degraded network scenarios; (2) Latency impact remains within customer-defined budget; (3) Mechanisms can be independently enabled/disabled and tuned without redesign.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates network communication protocols, QoS management, retransmission logic, and forward error correction for command and sensor data streams between operator and vehicle

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and high-level task directives; Teleoperation module applies QoS prioritization and selective retransmission to critical tasking payloads

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs sensor streams with FEC encoding applied; Teleoperation module manages sensor data QoS and reliability guarantees

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical alerts from Safety Services; Teleoperation applies highest QoS priority and mandatory retransmission; may echo back acknowledgment

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts underlying network transport; Teleoperation module configures link-layer QoS parameters and monitors path quality for adaptive FEC/retransmission decisions

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation is the natural owner of command/sensor transport, QoS prioritization, and retransmission logic. Hybrid approach (selective retransmission + FEC + QoS) is core to teleoperation reliability. Minor diffusion: Safety Services must define criticality thresholds for command classification; Vehicle must expose sensor stream metadata for FEC adaptation.

Poor fit: Safety Services owns safety policy and alert generation, not network transport mechanics. Placing hybrid network protocol logic here violates separation of concerns. FEC, retransmission, and QoS tuning are transport-layer concerns, not safety-policy concerns. Would require Safety Services to depend heavily on Network Link and Vehicle internals.

Poor fit: Vehicle owns actuators, sensors, and local control logic. Network protocol implementation (retransmission, FEC, QoS) is not a vehicle responsibility; it belongs in the communication layer. Placing DP5 here would couple vehicle firmware to network transport details and create tight coupling to Teleoperation logic.

Moderate fit but too broad: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it could logically own all communication infrastructure, it is not a specific module boundary—it is the system itself. Assigning DP5 here provides no architectural clarity on ownership, interfaces, or deployment packaging.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end command and sensor stream reliability. It sits at the boundary between operator intent (Tasking) and vehicle state (Telemetry), and is responsible for ensuring that commands reach the vehicle with appropriate redundancy and that sensor data reaches the operator with acceptable latency and fidelity. QoS prioritization, selective retransmission for critical commands, and FEC for sensor streams are all transport-layer concerns that belong in Teleoperation. The module will depend on Network Link to configure and monitor the physical path, and will coordinate with Safety Services to classify command criticality.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become large if it also owns protocol state machines, codec management, and adaptive algorithm tuning. Mitigation: Decompose into sub-modules (e.g., QoS Manager, Retransmission Handler, FEC Encoder/Decoder) if complexity grows.

Risk: Safety Services must provide clear criticality labels for commands so Teleoperation can apply selective retransmission. If Safety Services does not expose this metadata, Teleoperation will have to infer it, creating hidden coupling. Mitigation: Define explicit interface contract (e.g., Tasking payload includes criticality field).

Risk: Network Link abstraction must expose sufficient monitoring data (packet loss, latency, jitter) for Teleoperation to adapt FEC and retransmission parameters. If Network Link is opaque, DP5 cannot be fully realized. Mitigation: Extend Network Link interface to include path quality metrics.

Risk: Vehicle sensor streams must include metadata (frame rate, payload size, tolerance for loss) so Teleoperation can tune FEC overhead. If Vehicle does not expose this, Teleoperation will apply one-size-fits-all FEC, reducing efficiency. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry interface to include stream metadata.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target). The Teleoperation IM boundary is responsible for end-to-end reliability measurement and reporting. Verification can be conducted via network simulation, field testing under controlled packet loss injection, and operational telemetry collection. The module's interfaces (Tasking in, Telemetry out, SafetyAlert inout, Network Link inout) provide clear observation points for packet loss metrics.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. The FR is allocated to the Teleoperation module with explicit ownership of command and sensor stream reliability. The module's scope encompasses network communication protocols, QoS management, retransmission logic, and FEC—all necessary to meet the packet loss criterion. External dependencies (Safety Services for criticality labels, Network Link for path monitoring, Vehicle for sensor metadata) are identified and documented.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid approach: selective retransmission + FEC + QoS prioritization) is a sound and complete solution to the FR. It addresses packet loss through three complementary mechanisms: (1) selective retransmission ensures critical commands are reliably delivered; (2) FEC protects sensor streams against burst loss without requiring round-trip latency; (3) QoS prioritization ensures safety-critical traffic is serviced first. The DP is technology-agnostic and adaptable to various network conditions. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: MODERATE DIFFUSION: Criticality classification logic. DP5 requires commands to be labeled as 'critical' or 'non-critical' to apply selective retransmission. This classification is owned by Safety Services (safety policy domain), but Teleoperation must consume it. Mitigation: Establish explicit interface contract on Tasking payload to include a criticality field (e.g., enum: SAFETY_CRITICAL, MISSION_CRITICAL, BEST_EFFORT). Document ownership boundary: Safety Services defines policy; Teleoperation applies transport strategy.

MODERATE DIFFUSION: Adaptive FEC tuning. DP5 requires FEC parameters (code rate, block size) to adapt based on network path quality and sensor stream characteristics. Network Link owns path monitoring; Vehicle owns sensor metadata. Teleoperation must integrate signals from both. Mitigation: Extend Network Link interface to expose real-time metrics (packet loss rate, latency, jitter); extend Telemetry interface to include stream metadata (frame rate, payload size, loss tolerance). Teleoperation becomes the orchestrator, not the owner of underlying data.

LOW DIFFUSION: Protocol state machine complexity. If Teleoperation grows to include detailed protocol state machines, codec management, and buffer management, it may become monolithic. Mitigation: Plan for sub-module decomposition (e.g., QoS Manager, Retransmission Handler, FEC Codec Wrapper) if complexity exceeds maintainability threshold. Ensure sub-modules remain within Teleoperation boundary and do not leak into Safety Services or Vehicle.

LOW DIFFUSION: Network Link abstraction opacity. If Network Link does not expose sufficient monitoring data, Teleoperation cannot adapt DP5 parameters dynamically. Mitigation: Validate Network Link interface contract early; ensure it includes path quality metrics and configuration hooks for QoS parameters.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural and correct owner of DP5. The module sits at the communication boundary between operator and vehicle, making it the logical place to implement end-to-end reliability mechanisms. The hybrid approach (selective retransmission + FEC + QoS) is cohesive and transport-layer-specific. Alternative allocations (Safety Services, Vehicle, NGHE Solution) were evaluated and rejected due to separation-of-concerns violations and architectural coupling risks. Teleoperation's external interfaces are well-defined and support the required dependencies (criticality from Safety Services, path metrics from Network Link, sensor metadata from Vehicle). No significant architectural misalignment detected.

recommended_revisions: INTERFACE CONTRACT: Extend Tasking interface specification to mandate a criticality field in command payloads. Define enum values (SAFETY_CRITICAL, MISSION_CRITICAL, BEST_EFFORT) and document how Teleoperation applies selective retransmission thresholds based on criticality. Assign ownership: Safety Services defines policy; Teleoperation applies transport strategy.

INTERFACE CONTRACT: Extend Network Link interface to expose real-time path quality metrics (instantaneous packet loss rate, latency percentiles, jitter). Define update frequency and accuracy requirements so Teleoperation can adapt FEC and retransmission parameters with sufficient responsiveness.

INTERFACE CONTRACT: Extend Telemetry interface to include stream metadata (frame rate, payload size, loss tolerance, codec type). Document how Vehicle exposes this metadata and how Teleoperation uses it to tune FEC overhead and buffering strategies.

DECOMPOSITION PLAN: If Teleoperation module complexity exceeds 5000 LOC or involves more than 3 distinct protocol state machines, plan for sub-module decomposition (e.g., QoS Manager, Retransmission Handler, FEC Codec Wrapper). Ensure sub-modules remain within Teleoperation boundary and do not create hidden dependencies on Safety Services or Vehicle internals.

VERIFICATION PLAN: Define acceptance test cases for packet loss criterion under controlled network conditions (e.g., 5%, 10%, 15% injected loss). Specify measurement methodology (packet capture, end-to-end latency, command delivery confirmation). Assign responsibility to Teleoperation module for telemetry reporting.

SAFETY COORDINATION: Document interaction between Teleoperation and Safety Services for SafetyAlert handling. Specify that SafetyAlert payloads receive highest QoS priority and mandatory retransmission, and that Teleoperation must acknowledge receipt. Define timeout and escalation behavior if SafetyAlert delivery fails.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR5 is well-formed, DP5 is logically sound, and Teleoperation IM is the correct owner with excellent containment (0.92 score). However, the recommended_revisions identify critical interface contracts and decomposition thresholds that must be resolved before proceeding to detailed design. The moderate diffusion risks (criticality classification, adaptive FEC tuning) and the explicit decomposition plan contingency indicate that FR5 is ready for sub-level functional decomposition. The high safety criticality tailoring factor further justifies drilling down to ensure reliability mechanisms are fully specified at the sub-FR level. Proceeding to next-level decomposition will operationalize the DP5 hybrid approach and establish clear ownership boundaries for criticality labeling, path monitoring integration, and protocol state machine management.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Classify incoming commands by criticality level (SAFETY_CRITICAL, MISSION_CRITICAL, BEST_EFFORT) based on Safety Services policy and apply selective retransmission thresholds accordingly.
FR5.2: Monitor real-time network path quality metrics (packet loss rate, latency, jitter) from Network Link and adapt FEC code rate and retransmission timeout dynamically.
FR5.3: Retrieve sensor stream metadata (frame rate, payload size, loss tolerance, codec type) from Vehicle and use it to tune FEC overhead and buffer allocation per stream.
FR5.4: Implement selective retransmission logic for SAFETY_CRITICAL and MISSION_CRITICAL commands with configurable retry limits and timeout escalation to SafetyAlert.
FR5.5: Implement Forward Error Correction (FEC) encoding/decoding for sensor telemetry streams with adaptive code rate based on path quality and stream characteristics.
FR5.6: Implement QoS prioritization queue that services SafetyAlert and SAFETY_CRITICAL traffic first, followed by MISSION_CRITICAL, then BEST_EFFORT, with per-queue congestion management.
FR5.7: Measure and report end-to-end packet loss rate for command and telemetry streams, with per-criticality-level granularity, to support verification of ≤1% acceptance criterion.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but requires explicit operationalization of the three mechanisms (selective retransmission, FEC, QoS prioritization) at the sub-FR level. The recommended_revisions mandate interface contracts for criticality field, path metrics, and stream metadata. These contracts should be reflected in DP5 as explicit design assumptions and interface specifications before sub-FR decomposition proceeds. Additionally, the decomposition plan contingency (sub-module structure if complexity exceeds threshold) should be documented as a DP5 refinement to guide architectural packaging decisions.

assumptions: Safety Services will provide criticality classification policy and expose it via Tasking interface with a mandatory criticality field (enum: SAFETY_CRITICAL, MISSION_CRITICAL, BEST_EFFORT).
Network Link will expose real-time path quality metrics (packet loss rate, latency percentiles, jitter) with update frequency sufficient for adaptive FEC and retransmission tuning (assumed ≥1 Hz).
Vehicle will expose sensor stream metadata (frame rate, payload size, loss tolerance, codec type) via Telemetry interface or a dedicated metadata channel.
SafetyAlert payloads are always SAFETY_CRITICAL and receive mandatory retransmission and highest QoS priority; Teleoperation must acknowledge receipt and escalate timeout failures.

Teleoperation module will remain within a single architectural boundary; sub-module decomposition (QoS Manager, Retransmission Handler, FEC Codec Wrapper) is planned only if complexity exceeds 5000 LOC or 3+ distinct protocol state machines.

Packet loss measurement is conducted end-to-end at Teleoperation module boundary using packet capture, command delivery confirmation, and operational telemetry; no reliance on Network Link internal metrics for acceptance criterion verification.
open_risks: MODERATE: Criticality classification diffusion. If Safety Services policy changes or is ambiguous, Teleoperation may apply inconsistent retransmission thresholds. Mitigation: Establish formal interface contract with Safety Services; define policy versioning and change notification mechanism.

MODERATE: Adaptive FEC tuning responsiveness. If Network Link metrics are stale or infrequent, Teleoperation cannot adapt FEC parameters in time to prevent burst loss. Mitigation: Validate Network Link update frequency and latency; define fallback FEC parameters for metric unavailability.

MODERATE: Sub-module decomposition scope creep. If Teleoperation sub-modules (QoS Manager, Retransmission Handler, FEC Codec Wrapper) are not carefully scoped, they may leak dependencies into Safety Services or Vehicle. Mitigation: Define sub-module boundaries and interface contracts early; enforce architectural review before implementation.

LOW: Protocol state machine complexity. If retransmission, FEC, and QoS logic interact in unexpected ways, state machine may become intractable. Mitigation: Use formal verification or model-based testing for protocol correctness; plan for incremental implementation and integration testing.

LOW: Network Link abstraction opacity. If Network Link does not expose sufficient monitoring data or configuration hooks, Teleoperation cannot fully implement DP5. Mitigation: Validate Network Link interface contract early; escalate to architecture review if gaps are identified.
evidence_needed: Formal interface contract specification for Tasking payload (criticality field, enum values, semantics).
Formal interface contract specification for Network Link path quality metrics (packet loss rate, latency percentiles, jitter, update frequency, accuracy).
Formal interface contract specification for Telemetry stream metadata (frame rate, payload size, loss tolerance, codec type, exposure mechanism).
SafetyAlert handling specification: QoS priority, retransmission policy, acknowledgment mechanism, timeout and escalation behavior.
Acceptance test plan for packet loss criterion (≤1% or customer-defined target) with controlled network conditions (5%, 10%, 15% injected loss), measurement methodology, and pass/fail criteria.
Complexity threshold definition and sub-module decomposition plan (if Teleoperation exceeds 5000 LOC or 3+ protocol state machines).
Network Link interface validation report confirming sufficient monitoring data and configuration hooks for adaptive FEC and retransmission tuning.
Safety Services policy documentation on criticality classification and change notification mechanism.
